title: Digital Services Security Controls
verbatim:
Securing digital services is an integral part of financial institution’s risk management. A financial institution must expand its CRF to implement robust technology security controls in providing digital services which assure the following:
(a) adopt and regularly assess the minimum-security controls for respective delivery channels to ensure confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty information and transactions. Refer to Appendices 2, 3, 4 and 11;
(b) proper authentication of users or devices and authorization of transactions to mitigate impersonation and fraud risk. Refer the minimum-security controls in Appendix 3; and
(c) strong physical control and logical control measures.
translation: A financial institution must extend its Cyber Risk Framework (CRF) to implement robust security controls across all digital services. This requires regularly assessing minimum security controls for each delivery channel to protect the confidentiality and integrity of customer and counterparty information and transactions, properly authenticating users or devices and authorising transactions to mitigate impersonation and fraud risk, and maintaining strong physical and logical access controls.
evidence: Security control implementation evidence per channel

artifacts.description: Standards defining security requirements for all digital channels

artifacts.description: Security architecture documentation for digital banking channels

title: Alternative Controls and Liability
verbatim:
In the event that a financial institution has not yet implemented the digital services security controls specified under paragraph 12.1 to 12.9, the financial institution must-
(a) be ready to provide documented explanation of how alternatives measures or mitigations achieve equivalent or superior effectiveness; and
(b) assume the liability of any fraud that occurs due to the gaps arise from the absence of the specified control.
translation: Where a financial institution has not yet implemented the digital services security controls specified in paragraphs 12.1 to 12.9, it must be prepared to provide documented evidence showing how its alternative measures achieve equivalent or superior effectiveness. Additionally, the institution must assume full liability for any fraud that occurs as a result of gaps arising from the absence of the specified controls.
evidence: App store release security sign-off records

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: CRF Enhancement for Digital Fraud
translation: A financial institution must enhance its CRF to address digital fraud by expanding threat identification to include customers' mobile devices and access points, and by applying layered (defense-in-depth) controls to protect digital service applications and the banking data they contain. The institution must conduct continuous surveillance to detect exploitation of mobile applications, maintain clearly defined incident handling procedures to assist affected customers, and formalise cross-functional response arrangements for rapid countermeasure deployment. Senior management must regularly review the effectiveness of digital fraud management and escalate findings to the board to preserve public confidence and mitigate reputational risk.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Fraud Detection Capabilities
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that its fraud detection capabilities and rules are updated in a timely manner upon detection of new fraud modus operandi in order to prevent fraudulent transactions or account takeover using stolen customer credentials. This must be supported by appropriate risk analytics to improve the accuracy of fraud detection, that includes continuous upgrade of fraud detection capabilities as specified in Appendix 11 on Fraud Detection Standards.
translation: A financial institution must update its fraud detection capabilities and rules promptly whenever a new fraud modus operandi is detected, in order to prevent fraudulent transactions or account takeover using stolen customer credentials. This obligation is supported by a requirement to apply appropriate risk analytics and to continuously upgrade fraud detection capabilities in accordance with the Fraud Detection Standards set out in Appendix 11.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Digital Service Delivery Risk Mitigation
verbatim:
A financial institution must mitigate any attendant risk arising from its delivery of digital services. This shall include-
(a) adopt secure communication channel to mitigate risk of phishing. Compensating controls shall be adopted when using communication channel prone to phishing exploits;
(b) all customers must be properly informed in advance of new controls to ease adoption and minimise inconvenience; and
(c) practical ways for customers to verify the authenticity of calls made by the financial institution or its appointed outsourced service providers.
translation: A financial institution must mitigate risks arising from its delivery of digital services by adopting secure communication channels to reduce phishing risk, and applying compensating controls where the channel used is prone to phishing exploits. All customers must be properly informed in advance of any new controls, and the institution must provide practical ways for customers to verify the authenticity of calls made by the institution or its appointed outsourced service providers.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Fraud Risk Empowerment
verbatim: A financial institution shall empower customers to mitigate digital fraud risks where it is reasonably practical. This shall include offering customer mechanisms to manage activation and de-activation of payment card for card-not-present and overseas transactions. This shall not absolve the financial institution from its liabilities, responsibilities and duty of care to ensure the security of its digital services.
translation: A financial institution must empower customers to manage their own digital fraud risks where it is reasonably practical to do so, including by offering mechanisms to activate and deactivate payment cards for card-not-present and overseas transactions. Providing these customer controls does not relieve the institution of its own liabilities, responsibilities, or duty of care to ensure the security of its digital services.

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Fraud Awareness Programmes
translation: A financial institution must continuously review and enhance the effectiveness of its customer fraud awareness programmes to ensure customers understand the risks of digital services fraud. This includes providing timely and practical information on how to identify potential fraud and specific new or common modus operandi, clearly explaining new and existing security measures, issuing real-time alerts when security measures are absent or not yet implemented, and using measures such as interactive simulations to further improve customer understanding of fraud risks and controls.
evidence: Fraud detection system architecture and configuration

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Customer Account Suspension and Kill Switch
verbatim:
A financial institution must provide convenient means for customers to report, suspend and re-activate their account swiftly in the event of a suspected fraud, where applicable. This shall require the financial institution to:
(a) offer a secure self-service “kill switch” solution;
(b) ensure that its contact centre is adequately resourced and operating effectively to provide prompt and adequate assistance to customers in distress; and
(c) restore customer access to digital services within a reasonable timeframe upon validation.
translation: A financial institution must provide customers with convenient means to report a suspected fraud and to swiftly suspend and re-activate their accounts where applicable. This requires the institution to offer a secure self-service kill switch solution, ensure its contact centre is adequately resourced and operating effectively to assist customers in distress, and restore customer access to digital services within a reasonable timeframe following validation.
evidence: Liveness detection and document verification evidence

subsection: Digital Fraud Management and Customer Awareness
title: Post-Incident Customer Protection
verbatim:
In addition to effective incident handling procedures, a financial institution must adopt additional measures to mitigate risk to customers arising from any incident involving potential fraud or compromise of customer data. This shall include-
(a) heighten monitoring of affected customer accounts;
(b) notify affected customers and provide them with the necessary information to apply mitigating measures and reduce the risk of fraud; and
(c) revoke and re-issue affected user credentials or designated payment instruments, where there is a potential risk of exploitation due to compromised data or other fraudulent activity.
translation: In addition to its incident handling procedures, a financial institution must adopt further measures to protect customers affected by any incident involving potential fraud or compromise of customer data. These measures include heightening monitoring of affected accounts, notifying affected customers and providing them with the information needed to apply mitigating measures, and revoking and re-issuing affected user credentials or payment instruments where there is a potential risk of exploitation from compromised data or other fraudulent activity.
